key_points: Gravitational force is a universal attractive force acting between all masses in the universe
Newton's universal law of gravitation explains planetary motion, tides and falling bodies
Acceleration due to gravity is derived from the equation g = GM/R²
The value of g varies with altitude, depth and latitude due to Earth's shape and rotation
Weight of an object is directly proportional to mass and gravitational acceleration
Pressure exerted by a body is inversely proportional to the area of contact
Buoyant force arises due to pressure differences in fluids
Archimedes' principle states that buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced fluid
Floating condition occurs when buoyant force equals the weight of the object
Density plays a major role in determining whether an object floats, sinks or remains suspended
examples: Satellites remain in orbit due to gravitational attraction of Earth
Ocean tides caused by gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun
Wide tyres in vehicles reducing pressure on the ground
Dams built thicker at the bottom to withstand higher water pressure
formulas: Universal law of gravitation → F = G(Mm)/r²
Acceleration due to gravity → g = GM/R²
Buoyant force → Weight of displaced fluid
exam_tips: Remember inverse square relation of gravitational force with distance
Be able to derive g = GM/R² from Newton's law
Understand differences between mass and weight conceptually
Use Archimedes' principle to explain floating and sinking problems
Link density and buoyancy concepts in numerical questions
